briefdescription.para: Size is an alias of Dali::Vector2. @SINCE_1_0.0.
Position is an alias of Dali::Vector2. @SINCE_2_5.18.
Scale is an alias of Dali::Vector2. @SINCE_2_5.18.
Typedef for a viewport ( a rectangle representing a screen area ). @SINCE_1_0.0.
Compares two angle axis for equality.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
lhs
angle axis
rhs
angle axis
True if they are equal

briefdescription.para: Compares equality between two degrees.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
lhs
Degree to compare
rhs
Degree to compare to
True if the values are identical

briefdescription.para: Compares inequality between two degrees.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
lhs
Degree to compare
rhs
Degree to compare to
True if the values are not identical

briefdescription.para: Clamps a radian value. @SINCE_1_0.0.
Returns the next power of two.

detaileddescription.para:
In case of numbers which are already a power of two this function returns the original number. If i is zero returns 1. @SINCE_1_0.0
i
Input number
The next power of two or i itself in case it's a power of two

briefdescription.para: Whether a number is power of two.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
i
Input number
True if i is power of two.

briefdescription.para: Clamp a value.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
value
The value to clamp.
min
The minimum allowed value.
max
The maximum allowed value.
T the clamped value

briefdescription.para: Clamp a value directly.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
value
The value that will be clamped.
min
The minimum allowed value.
max
The maximum allowed value.

memberdef.argsstring: (const float offset, const T &low, const T &high)
memberdef.name: Lerp

briefdescription.para: Linear interpolation between two values.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
offset
The offset through the range low to high. This value is clamped between 0 and 1.
low
Lowest value in range
high
Highest value in range
A value between low and high.

briefdescription.para: Get an epsilon that is valid for the given range.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
a
the first value in the range
b
the second value in the range.
a suitable epsilon

briefdescription.para: Helper function to compare equality of a floating point value with zero.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
value
the value to compare
true if the value is equal to zero

briefdescription.para: Helper function to compare equality of two floating point values.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
a
the first value to compare
b
the second value to compare
true if the values are equal within a minimal epsilon for their values

briefdescription.para: Helper function to compare equality of two floating point values.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
a
the first value to compare
b
the second value to compare
epsilon
the minimum epsilon value that will be used to consider the values different
true if the difference between the values is less than the epsilon

briefdescription.para: Get an float that is rounded at specified place of decimals.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
value
float value
pos
decimal place
a rounded float

briefdescription.para: Wrap x in domain (start) to (end).

detaileddescription.para: This works like a floating point version of the % modulo operation. But with an offset (start).
For instance a domain is specified as: start: 2 end: 8
The value x will be confined to this domain. If x is below 2 e.g. 0, then it is wrapped to 6. If x is above or equal to 8 e.g. 8.1 then it is wrapped to 2.1.
Domain wrapping is useful for various problems from calculating positions in a space that repeats, to computing angles that range from 0 to 360.

@SINCE_1_0.0
x
the point to be wrapped within the domain
start
The start of the domain
end
The end of the domain
the wrapped value over the domain (start) (end)
If start = end (i.e. size of domain 0), then wrapping will not occur and result will always be equal to start.

memberdef.argsstring: (float a, float b, float start, float end)
memberdef.name: ShortestDistanceInDomain

briefdescription.para: Find the shortest distance (magnitude) and direction (sign) from (a) to (b) in domain (start) to (end).

detaileddescription.para: Knowing the shortest distance is useful with wrapped domains to solve problems such as determining the closest object to a given point, or determining whether turning left or turning right is the shortest route to get from angle 10 degrees to angle 350 degrees (clearly in a 0-360 degree domain, turning left 20 degrees is quicker than turning right 340 degrees).

The value returned holds the distance and the direction from value a to value b. For instance in the above example it would return -20. i.e. subtract 20 from current value (10) to reach target wrapped value (350).

@SINCE_1_0.0
a
the current value
b
the target value
start
the start of the domain
end
the end of the domain
the shortest direction (the sign) and distance (the magnitude)
Assumes both (a) and (b) are already within the domain (start) to (end).

briefdescription.para: Extracts the sign of a number.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
value
The value we want to extract the sign
-1 for negative values, +1 for positive values and 0 if value is 0

briefdescription.para: Returns a vector with components set to the minimum of the corresponding component in a and b.

detaileddescription.para:
If a=0,1 and b=1,0 returns a vector of 0,0. @SINCE_1_0.0
a
A vector
b
A vector
A vector containing the minimum of each component from a and b

briefdescription.para: Returns a vector with components set to the maximum of the corresponding component in a and b.

detaileddescription.para:
If a=0,1 and b=1,0 returns a vector of 1,1. @SINCE_1_0.0
a
A vector
b
A vector
A vector containing the maximum of each component from a and b

briefdescription.para: Clamps each of vector v's components between minimum and maximum values.

detaileddescription.para:
@SINCE_1_0.0
v
A vector
min
The minimum value
max
The maximum value
A vector containing the clamped components of v

briefdescription.para: Returns a vector with components set to the minimum of the corresponding component in a and b.

detaileddescription.para:
If a=0,1,2 and b=2,1,0 returns a vector of 2,1,2. @SINCE_1_0.0
a
A vector
b
A vector
A vector containing the minimum of each component from a and b

briefdescription.para: Returns a vector with components set to the maximum of the corresponding component in a and b.

detaileddescription.para:
If a=0,1 and b=1,0 returns a vector of 1,1. @SINCE_1_0.0
a
A vector
b
A vector
A vector containing the maximum of each component from a and b

v

briefdescription.para: Returns a vector with components set to the minimum of the corresponding component in a and b.

detaileddescription.para:
If a=0,1,2,3 and b=4,0,1,2 returns a vector of 0,0,1,2. @SINCE_1_0.0
a
A vector
b
A vector
A vector containing the minimum of each component from a and b

briefdescription.para: Returns a vector with components set to the maximum of the corresponding component in a and b.

detaileddescription.para:
If a=0,1,2,3 and b=4,0,1,2 returns a vector of 4,1,2,3. @SINCE_1_0.0
a
A vector
b
A vector
A vector containing the maximum of each component from a and b
